title: Why the style is not applied
why.empty: The editor is empty. Hit the bin and start over.
why.untouched: The code is exactly as it was.
why.unlinked: The page cannot find styles.css. You changed the link line.
hint2.unlinked: Hit the bin and start over.
why.used-inline: You used style in the tag. That is lesson 01 again.
why.touched-html: You edited the HTML. It is correct.
why.no-lead: There is no element with the lead class.
why.no-card: There is no element with the card class.
why.no-card-p: There is no paragraph inside the card.
why.typo-left: The misspelled class name is still there.
hint2.typo-left: The rule is written correctly and matches nothing. Compare its name with the one in the HTML.
why.lead-overridden: The lead sentence is not brown.
hint2.lead-overridden: Its rule exists and its value is valid. So something is taking it away.
hint3.lead-overridden: Look for a SECOND rule for the same class. At equal strength the last one written wins — and the bottom one is the bottom one, even when you forgot it was there.
hint4.lead-overridden: Delete the second .lead rule at the bottom.
why.card-plain: The card has no border and no padding.
hint2.card-plain: Its rule exists but matches no element at all.
why.size-wrong: The card text is not larger than the rest.
hint2.size-wrong: Check the unit. The course insisted that text sizes be written in rem.
why.h1-broken: The heading is no longer brown.
blocks.0.text: Three things on this page do not look right. All three rules are in the file, all written without error, and the console is silent. But for three different reasons.
blocks.2.items.0: The rule matches nothing. The selector is valid as syntax, but no such element exists — a misspelling, a missing dot, an extra space.
blocks.2.items.1: The value is invalid. The browser steps over the line and keeps the previous value — a missing percent sign, the wrong unit, a variable name that does not exist.
blocks.2.items.2: The rule works, but another one beats it. Both apply, one wins, and what you see on screen is the other value.
blocks.4.text: You already know the first two from the broken-code lessons. The third is the new one and the most confusing, because your rule is NOT wrong — it simply is not the last word. You recognise it like this: if the element has some definite value, just not yours, then someone else is speaking louder or later.
blocks.5.text: For now one rule is enough: at equal strength the last one written wins. That is exactly why a second rule for the same class, forgotten at the end of the file, cancels the first without leaving a trace. Who wins at DIFFERENT strengths is the next lesson and a longer story.
blocks.6.text: A style that is not applied is one of three cases. Ask which one before you touch anything.
blocks.8.text: First: does such an element exist at all — open the HTML and look for the name letter by letter. Second: is the value valid — does it have a unit, does the variable exist. Third: is there a rule further down for the same thing. Those three questions cover almost everything you will meet, and they go in that order because the first two take seconds to check.
blocks.10.text: Fix the three things: the lead sentence should be brown, the card should have a border and padding, and its text should be larger than the rest. Each of the three is a different reason.
